     6â†’
     7â†’# Bash Console Guide (BCG) - Pattern Reference
     8â†’
     9â†’## Core Philosophy
    10â†’
    11â†’This pattern emerged from environments where hidden failures cascade into production outages. When bash scripts manage critical infrastructure, every potential failure point needs explicit handling at its location, making post-mortem analysis straightforward.
    12â†’
    13â†’The aesthetic of elegant bash pipelines trades debuggability for brevity. A failing command in `$(echo | sed | awk)` leaves no trace. Temp files make each transformation step visible, testable, and recoverable.
    14â†’
    15â†’Traps and signal handlers create action-at-a-distance effects that obscure control flow. When error handling lives at the point of failure rather than in distant handlers, code review and maintenance become predictable.
    16â†’
    17â†’Enterprise bash isn't about looking clever; it's about being boringly, reliably correct. These patterns optimize for the 3am debugging session, not the initial writing experience. Each external process spawn is a potential failure point, so bash builtins are preferred despite verbosity.
    18â†’
    19â†’The patterns reject bash 4+ features to maintain compatibility with older enterprise systems still running bash 3.2, particularly older RHEL/CentOS or macOS deployments.
    20â†’
    21â†’## Module Architecture
    22â†’
    23â†’Every module has an implementation file. CLI entry points are only present if module requires standalone execution.
    24â†’
    25â†’```bash
    26â†’Â«prefixÂ»_Â«nameÂ».sh (implementation - REQUIRED, prefer single lowercase word, allow snake_case)
    27â†’Â«prefixÂ»_cli.sh (executable entry point - OPTIONAL, omit for library/utility modules)
    28â†’```
    29â†’
    30â†’### CLI as Module Gateway
    31â†’
    32â†’**Rule**: Code outside the BCG module system must never call `z*_kindle()` directly. All external access to BCG modules goes through CLI scripts.
    33â†’
    34â†’**Pattern**:
    35â†’```
    36â†’External Code â†’ CLI script â†’ furnish() kindles â†’ module functions
    37â†’```
    38â†’
    39â†’**Why this matters**:
    40â†’- Kindle sequences are implementation details that may change
    41â†’- The CLI's `furnish()` owns the kindle graph (which modules to kindle, in what order)
    42â†’- External code remains stable when module dependencies change
    43â†’- CLIs provide the documented, stable API for module functionality
    44â†’
    45â†’**Anti-pattern**:
    46â†’```bash
    47â†’# âťŚ External script kindling directly
    48â†’source "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_bottle.sh"
    49â†’zrbob_kindle                          # VIOLATION: kindle leaked outside CLI
    50â†’rbob_start
    51â†’```
    52â†’
    53â†’**Correct pattern**:
    54â†’```bash
    55â†’# âś… External script delegates to CLI
    56â†’exec "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_cli.sh" rbob_start "$@"
    57â†’```
    58â†’
    59â†’**What counts as "external code"**:
    60â†’- Entry point scripts (workbenches, testbenches, coordinators)
    61â†’- TabTargets and launchers
    62â†’- Any dispatcher or router that invokes module functionality
    63â†’- Scripts that are not themselves BCG modules
    64â†’
    65â†’**What can call kindle**:
    66â†’- A module's own CLI (in `furnish()`)
    67â†’- Another CLI's `furnish()` (when CLI A depends on module B, A's furnish kindles B)
    68â†’- Test harnesses that explicitly isolate module behavior
    69â†’
    70â†’### Module-CLI Prefix Unity
    71â†’
    72â†’**Convention**: A module and its CLI share a prefix (`rbrn_regime.sh` + `rbrn_cli.sh`).
    73â†’
    74â†’When commands within a single CLI have different dependency weights (e.g., `validate` needs
    75â†’only the regime file, `audit` needs GCP/OAuth), use **differential furnish**: the furnish
    76â†’function receives the command name as `$1` and conditionally sources/kindles heavy
    77â†’dependencies based on which command is being invoked.
    78â†’
    79â†’### Regime Module Archetype
    80â†’
    81â†’A **regime** is a sourced `.env` file containing typed configuration fields â€” the contract between human-authored configuration and the modules that consume it. When a module's purpose is validating a regime, it follows the regime archetype:
    82â†’
    83â†’| Component | Location | Purpose |
    84â†’|-----------|----------|---------|
    85â†’| `buv_*_enroll` calls | kindle | Declare field contracts (type, range, description) |
    86â†’| `buv_scope_sentinel` | kindle (last, before KINDLED) | Catch undeclared `PREFIX_*` variables |
    87â†’| `zÂ«prefixÂ»_enforce` | regime module | `buv_vet SCOPE` + custom format checks |
    88â†’| `*_validate` command | CLI | `buv_report` â€” explained per-field display |
    89â†’| `*_render` command | CLI | `buv_render` â€” diagnostic dump |
    90â†’
    91â†’Furnish sequence: kindle â†’ enforce (ironclad gate before any command runs).
    92â†’
    93â†’**Singleton** regimes source config unconditionally in furnish. **Manifold** regimes (multiple instances, e.g. nameplates) source conditionally when a folio identifies the instance.
    94â†’
    95â†’Enrollment types and gating API: see `buv_validation.sh`.
    96â†’
    97â†’---
    98â†’
    99â†’## Function Patterns
   100â†’
   101â†’### Boilerplate Functions (one per module/CLI)
   102â†’
   103â†’| Function             | Location       | First Line                                         | Can Source?       | Can use buc_step? | Purpose                                          |
   104â†’|----------------------|----------------|----------------------------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
   105â†’| `zÂ«prefixÂ»_kindle`   | Implementation | `test -z "${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_KINDLED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | Yes (credentials) | No (use buc_log_*)| Define all kindle constants, set module state    |
   106â†’| `zÂ«prefixÂ»_sentinel` | Implementation | `test "${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" \|\| buc_die` | No                | No                | Guard all other functions                        |
   107â†’| `zÂ«prefixÂ»_enforce`  | Implementation | `zÂ«prefixÂ»_sentinel`                               | No                | No                | Validate sourced config (regime archetype only)  |
   108â†’| `zÂ«prefixÂ»_furnish`  | CLI only       | `buc_doc_env...`                                   | Yes (configs)     | No                | Document env vars, source configs, call kindle   |
   109â†’| Module header        | Implementation | `test -z "${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_SOURCED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | No                | N/A               | Prevent multiple inclusion                       |
   110â†’| CLI header           | CLI            | `set -euo pipefail`                                | Yes (all deps)    | N/A               | Source all dependencies                          |
   111â†’
   112â†’**Kindle constant**: Any variable â€” internal (`ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_SCREAMING_NAME`) or public (`Â«PREFIXÂ»_SCREAMING_NAME`) â€” defined exclusively within the kindle function. No other function â€” including enroll, setup, or helper functions â€” may assign to kindle constants. This ensures module state is fully determined at kindle time and visible in one place.
   113â†’
   114â†’**Literal constant**: A public variable (`Â«PREFIXÂ»_lower_name`) defined at module top level, immediately after the `ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_SOURCED=1` guard. Literal constants must be pure string literals with **no variable expansion, no computation, and no runtime dependency**. They are available immediately after sourcing â€” no kindle required. Use `SCREAMING` case for the prefix (module identity) and `lower_snake` case for the name to visually distinguish from kindle constants. This enables sourcing chains where a literal constant from module A provides the path for sourcing module B's config in the same CLI header block.
   115â†’
   116â†’**KINDLED must be last**: `ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_KINDLED=1` must be the final statement in kindle. Since sentinel checks this variable, setting it last guarantees all kindle constants, roll arrays, and enroll calls are complete before the module is considered operational. Any function calling sentinel before kindle finishes will correctly fail.
   117â†’
   118â†’### Regular Functions (output via printouts/temp files)
   119â†’
   120â†’This table defines scope: Â«prefixÂ»_* is public, zÂ«prefixÂ»_* is internal.
   121â†’
   122â†’| Type            | Pattern              | First Line           | Parameters | buc_step | Documentation        |
   123â†’|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------|----------|----------------------|
   124â†’| Public          | `Â«prefixÂ»_Â«commandÂ»` | `zÂ«prefixÂ»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Required (buc_doc_*) |
   125â†’| Internal helper | `zÂ«prefixÂ»_Â«nameÂ»`   | `zÂ«prefixÂ»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Recommended (buc_doc_*) |
   126â†’
   127â†’---
   128â†’
   129â†’## File Templates
   130â†’
   131â†’### Template 1: Shebang and Copyright
   132â†’
   133â†’```bash
   134â†’#!/bin/bash
   135â†’#
   136â†’# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
   137â†’#
   138â†’# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
   139â†’# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
   140â†’# You may obtain a copy of the License at
   141â†’#
   142â†’#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
   143â†’#
   144â†’# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
   145â†’# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
   146â†’# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
   147â†’# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
   148â†’# limitations under the License.
   149â†’#
   150â†’# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
   151â†’#
   152â†’```
   153â†’
   154â†’### Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables
   155â†’
   156â†’Three BURD variables carry directory paths across the exec boundary into CLI processes. These are derived from BURC configuration and exported by bud_dispatch.sh during zbud_setup():
   157â†’
   158â†’| Variable | Source | Value |
   159â†’|----------|--------|-------|
   160â†’| `BURD_TOOLS_DIR` | `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | Project tools root directory |
   161â†’| `BURD_BUK_DIR` | Derived from `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | BUK subdirectory (`$BURC_TOOLS_DIR/buk`) |
   162â†’| `BURD_TABTARGET_DIR` | `BURC_TABTARGET_DIR` | Tabtarget directory |
   163â†’
   164â†’These variables are available in exec'd CLI processes without requiring the CLI to re-source BURC. CLIs that need stable paths to tools or utilities should use these variables rather than reconstructing paths from `BASH_SOURCE`.
   165â†’
   166â†’### Template 2: CLI Entry Point
   167â†’
   168â†’BUD variables are provided by dispatch. Validate and document only those used by the implementation.
   169â†’
   170â†’```bash
   171â†’# Â«shebangÂ»
   172â†’# Â«copyrightÂ»
   173â†’
   174â†’set -euo pipefail
   175â†’
   176â†’ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_SCRIPT_DIR="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"
   177â†’
   178â†’# Source all dependencies
   179â†’source "${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_CLI_SCRIPT_DIR}/buc_command.sh"
   180â†’source "${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_CLI_SCRIPT_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
   181â†’source "${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_CLI_SCRIPT_DIR}/Â«prefixÂ»_Â«nameÂ».sh"
   182â†’
   183â†’zÂ«prefixÂ»_furnish() {
   184â†’  
   185â†’  # Document only the BUD variables actually used
   186â†’  buc_doc_env "BURD_TEMP_DIR         " "Temporary directory for intermediate files"
   187â†’  buc_doc_env "BURD_NOW_STAMP        " "Unique string between invocations"
   188â†’  buc_doc_env "BURD_OUTPUT_DIR       " "Directory for command outputs"
   189â†’
   190â†’  # Document module specific environment variables needed
   191â†’  buc_doc_env "Â«PREFIXÂ»_XXX         " "Module specific environment variable"
   192â†’  buc_doc_env "Â«PREFIXÂ»_YYY         " "Module specific environment variable"
   193â†’  buc_doc_env "Â«PREFIXÂ»_REGIME_FILE " "Module specific configuration file"
   194â†’
   195â†’  # Source regime files, if any
   196â†’  source "${Â«PREFIXÂ»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime file"
   197â†’
   198â†’  zÂ«prefixÂ»_kindle
   199â†’  zÂ«prefixÂ»_enforce  # Regime archetype: ironclad gate after kindle (omit if no regime)
   200â†’}

200â†’}
   201â†’
   202â†’buc_execute Â«prefixÂ»_ "Â«module_description_phraseÂ»" zÂ«prefixÂ»_furnish "$@"
   203â†’
   204â†’# eof
   205â†’```
   206â†’
   207â†’### Differential Furnish (conditional dependency sourcing)
   208â†’
   209â†’The furnish function receives the **command name** as `$1`, enabling conditional sourcing based on which command will execute. This allows heavy dependencies (e.g., GCP client libraries, OAuth flows) to be loaded only for commands that need them.
   210â†’
   211â†’**Pattern**: Light dependencies always loaded; heavy dependencies conditionally sourced per command.
   212â†’
   213â†’```bash
   214â†’zÂ«prefixÂ»_furnish() {
   215â†’  local z_command="${1:-}"
   216â†’
   217â†’  # Document environment variables
   218â†’  buc_doc_env "BURD_TEMP_DIR         " "Temporary directory for intermediate files"
   219â†’  buc_doc_env "Â«PREFIXÂ»_REGIME_FILE " "Module configuration file"
   220â†’
   221â†’  # Always source light dependencies
   222â†’  source "${Â«PREFIXÂ»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime"
   223â†’  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source validation"
   224â†’
   225â†’  # Conditionally source heavy dependencies based on command
   226â†’  case "${z_command}" in
   227â†’    Â«prefixÂ»_survey|Â«prefixÂ»_audit)
   228â†’      # These commands need GCP and OAuth infrastructure
   229â†’      source "${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/some/heavy_dep.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source heavy deps"
   230â†’      zheavy_kindle
   231â†’      ;;
   232â†’    Â«prefixÂ»_validate|Â«prefixÂ»_render)
   233â†’      # These commands work with config only
   234â†’      ;;
   235â†’  esac
   236â†’
   237â†’  zÂ«prefixÂ»_kindle
   238â†’}
   239â†’```
   240â†’
   241â†’**Key points:**
   242â†’- The furnish function receives the command name (e.g., "Â«prefixÂ»_validate") as `$1`
   243â†’- Existing furnish functions that don't use `$1` are unaffected (extra arg ignored in bash)
   244â†’- Light, always-needed dependencies are sourced unconditionally
   245â†’- Heavy dependencies are sourced only for the commands that need them (avoiding startup overhead)
   246â†’- The `case` statement can be omitted if a module has no conditional dependencies
   247â†’
   248â†’**Benefits:**
   249â†’- Faster startup for lightweight commands (validate, render, list)
   250â†’- Heavy infrastructure (GCP SDKs, OAuth flows) loaded only when actually needed
   251â†’- Reduced memory footprint for simple operations
   252â†’- Clear visibility of which commands depend on what infrastructure
   253â†’
   254â†’### Template 3: Implementation Module
   255â†’
   256â†’```bash
   257â†’# Â«shebangÂ»
   258â†’# Â«copyrightÂ»
   259â†’
   260â†’set -euo pipefail
   261â†’
   262â†’# Multiple inclusion detection
   263â†’test -z "${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module Â«prefixÂ» multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
   264â†’ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_SOURCED=1
   265â†’
   266â†’# Literal constants (pure string literals, no variable expansion â€” available at source time)
   267â†’# Â«PREFIXÂ»_some_path="fixed-filename.env"
   268â†’# Â«PREFIXÂ»_some_prefix="prefix_"
   269â†’
   270â†’######################################################################
   271â†’# Internal Functions (zÂ«prefixÂ»_*)
   272â†’
   273â†’zÂ«prefixÂ»_kindle() {
   274â†’  test -z "${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module Â«prefixÂ» already kindled"
   275â†’
   276â†’  # Validate only the BURD variables actually used
   277â†’  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}"   # If using temp files
   278â†’  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"  # If producing outputs
   279â†’  test -n "${BURD_NOW_STAMP:-}" || buc_die "BURD_NOW_STAMP is unset"  # If using timestamps
   280â†’
   281â†’  # Validate module specific environment
   282â†’  buv_file_exists "${Â«PREFIXÂ»_REGIME_FILE}"  # If present
   283â†’
   284â†’  # Source credentials into environment only - never write to disk
   285â†’  source "${REGIME_CRED_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source credentials"
   286â†’
   287â†’  # Kindle constant (public â€” safe to read from other modules; never a secret)
   288â†’  Â«PREFIXÂ»_PUBLIC_SELECTED_NAME="some-specific-name"
   289â†’
   290â†’  # Kindle constants (internal)
   291â†’  ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_TEMP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/Â«prefixÂ»_temp.txt"  # Non-secret data only
   292â†’  ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_RESULT_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/Â«prefixÂ»_Â«commandÂ»_Â«step_numberÂ»_result.json"
   293â†’  ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_CONFIG_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/Â«prefixÂ»_Â«commandÂ»_Â«step_numberÂ»_config_"     # rest of file name appended on use
   294â†’  ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_MANIFEST_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/Â«prefixÂ»_Â«commandÂ»_Â«step_numberÂ»_manifest_"   # rest of file name appended on use
   295â†’
   296â†’  # Temporary files (if using BURD_OUTPUT_DIR)
   297â†’  ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_OUTPUT_RESULT="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/result.txt"
   298â†’
   299â†’  ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_KINDLED=1
   300â†’}
   301â†’
   302â†’zÂ«prefixÂ»_sentinel() {
   303â†’  test "${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module Â«prefixÂ» not kindled - call zÂ«prefixÂ»_kindle first"
   304â†’}
   305â†’
   306â†’# Helper functions follow
   307â†’
   308â†’######################################################################
   309â†’# External Functions (Â«prefixÂ»_*)
   310â†’
   311â†’Â«prefixÂ»_Â«commandÂ»() {
   312â†’  zÂ«prefixÂ»_sentinel
   313â†’
   314â†’  local z_param1="${1:-}"
   315â†’  local z_param2="${2:-}"
   316â†’
   317â†’  # Documentation block
   318â†’  buc_doc_brief "Â«one_line_descriptionÂ»"
   319â†’  buc_doc_param "Â«required_argÂ»" "Â«one_line_descriptionÂ»"
   320â†’  buc_doc_oparm "Â«optional_argÂ»" "Â«one_line_descriptionÂ»"
   321â†’  buc_doc_shown || return 0
   322â†’
   323â†’  # Implementation
   324â†’  test -n "${z_param1}" || buc_die "Parameter 'param1' is required"
   325â†’
   326â†’  buc_step "Â«user_visible_milestoneÂ»"
   327â†’  buc_log_args "Â«detailed_progress_informationÂ»"
   328â†’  [command] || buc_die "Â«failure_explanationÂ»"
   329â†’
   330â†’  buc_success "Â«success_messageÂ»"
   331â†’}
   332â†’```
   333â†’
   334â†’**Kindle constant â€” anti-pattern:**
   335â†’
   336â†’```bash
   337â†’# âťŚ Kindle constant defined outside kindle
   338â†’zÂ«prefixÂ»_setup() {
   339â†’  ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"   # VIOLATION: must be in kindle
   340â†’}
   341â†’
   342â†’# âś… Kindle constants defined in kindle, used elsewhere
   343â†’zÂ«prefixÂ»_kindle() {
   344â†’  ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"
   345â†’  ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_KINDLED=1
   346â†’}
   347â†’```
   348â†’
   349â†’**Literal vs kindle constant â€” choosing correctly:**
   350â†’
   351â†’```bash
   352â†’# âś… Literal constant: pure string, no variable expansion, available at source time
   353â†’Â«PREFIXÂ»_config_file="config.env"        # lower_snake name â€” no kindle needed
   354â†’
   355â†’# âś… Kindle constant: depends on runtime state, requires kindle
   356â†’Â«PREFIXÂ»_CONFIG_DIR="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # SCREAMING name â€” kindle required
   357â†’
   358â†’# âťŚ Wrong: variable expansion in literal constant position
   359â†’Â«PREFIXÂ»_config_dir="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # VIOLATION: uses ${}, must be in kindle
   360â†’```
   361â†’
   362â†’## Variable Handling (General Rules)
   363â†’
   364â†’### Expansion Requirements
   365â†’
   366â†’**Always use braced, quoted expansion:**
   367â†’```bash
   368â†’"${z_var}"              # Required pattern - prevents word splitting and glob expansion
   369â†’"${Z_MODULE_VAR}"       # Module variables from kindle
   370â†’"${1:-}"                # Parameters with defaults
   371â†’```
   372â†’
   373â†’**Never use:**
   374â†’```bash
   375â†’$var                    # Unquoted - word splitting risk
   376â†’"$var"                  # Unbraced - boundary ambiguity
   377â†’${var}                  # Unquoted - glob expansion risk
   378â†’```
   379â†’
   380â†’### Assignment Patterns
   381â†’
   382â†’```bash
   383â†’# Direct assignment
   384â†’local z_message="Processing item"
   385â†’
   386â†’# âťŚ NEVER declare multiple variables on one line
   387â†’local z_name z_value z_result  # Hidden initialization failures!
   388â†’
   389â†’# âś… ALWAYS one declaration per line with explicit initialization
   390â†’local z_name=""
   391â†’local z_value="default"
   392â†’local z_result=0
   393â†’
   394â†’# âťŚ NEVER use local -i â€” silently coerces non-integer values to 0
   395â†’local -i z_count="${z_input}"  # "abc" becomes 0 â€” violates no-silent-failures!
   396â†’
   397â†’# âś… Use plain local with explicit validation
   398â†’local z_count="${z_input}"
   399â†’test "${z_count}" -ge 0 2>/dev/null || buc_die "z_count must be non-negative integer, got: ${z_count}"

400â†’
   401â†’# âś… Two-line pattern for captured values (explicit and debuggable)
   402â†’local z_token
   403â†’z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
   404â†’
   405â†’# âťŚ NEVER combine declaration with capture
   406â†’local z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture)  # Hides exit status!
   407â†’
   408â†’# From file using builtin $(<file) - NOT command substitution, a bash builtin
   409â†’local z_content=$(<"${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_TEMP_FILE}")
   410â†’test -n "${z_content}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_TEMP_FILE}"
   411â†’
   412â†’# Never split declaration from $(<file) assignment
   413â†’# âťŚ local z_content
   414â†’# âťŚ z_content=$(<"${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_FILE}")  # Unnecessary verbosity
   415â†’
   416â†’# Use Capture functions for secrets that must never touch disk
   417â†’local z_token
   418â†’z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
   419â†’
   420â†’# Temp files INSTEAD of command substitution holds intermediate results
   421â†’echo "${z_result}" > "${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_RESULT_FILE}"
   422â†’complex_command > "${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
   423â†’process < "${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_TEMP1}" > "${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
   424â†’local z_result=$(<"${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_TEMP2}")
   425â†’test -n "${z_result}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_TEMP2}"
   426â†’```
   427â†’
   428â†’### Prefer Bash Builtins Over External Tools
   429â†’
   430â†’```bash
   431â†’# âś… String manipulation with parameter expansion
   432â†’"${z_path##*/}"                    # Instead of: basename
   433â†’"${z_path%/*}"                     # Instead of: dirname
   434â†’"${z_string//old/new}"             # Instead of: sed 's/old/new/g'
   435â†’"${z_string#prefix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/^prefix//'
   436â†’"${z_string%suffix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/suffix$//'
   437â†’
   438â†’# âś… Reading lines with 'read' â€” guard handles missing trailing newline
   439â†’while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
   440â†’  echo "Processing: ${z_line}" || buc_die "Failed to echo line"
   441â†’done < "${Z_MODULE_INPUT_FILE}"
   442â†’
   443â†’# âś… Arithmetic with $((...))
   444â†’z_result=$((z_value * 2 + 10))    # Instead of: expr or bc
   445â†’```
   446â†’
   447â†’### Temp Files Instead of Command Substitution
   448â†’
   449â†’```bash
   450â†’# âťŚ Anti-pattern: nested command substitution
   451â†’z_result=$(echo $(complex_command) | process)  # Hidden failures!
   452â†’
   453â†’# âś… Correct: temp files make failures visible afterward
   454â†’complex_command > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
   455â†’process < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
   456â†’read -r z_result < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "No result"
   457â†’
   458â†’# âš ď¸Ź EXCEPTION: Secrets use _capture functions, never temp files
   459â†’```
   460â†’
   461â†’**Temp file lifecycle**: Temp files under `BURD_TEMP_DIR` are **preserved after execution** for forensic debugging. Never delete temp files in module code â€” their persistence is intentional. Cleanup is handled by infrastructure outside BCG's scope.
   462â†’
   463â†’### Stderr Capture â€” Never Suppress
   464â†’
   465â†’Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands. Redirect stderr to a temp file so forensic evidence is preserved when commands fail. Include the stderr file path in the `buc_die` message so the user can inspect it.
   466â†’
   467â†’```bash
   468â†’# âťŚ Anti-pattern: stderr suppressed â€” failure cause invisible
   469â†’curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>/dev/null \
   470â†’  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url}"
   471â†’
   472â†’# âś… Correct: stderr captured to temp file, referenced in die message
   473â†’curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
   474â†’  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url} â€” see ${z_stderr_file}"
   475â†’```
   476â†’
   477â†’**Stderr file naming**: Use a kindle `_PREFIX` constant (grouped with other temp file prefixes for collision visibility) and append a discriminator at the usage site.
   478â†’
   479â†’**In loops**, use an auto-incrementing integer for uniqueness rather than trusting loop data values to be unique:
   480â†’
   481â†’```bash
   482â†’# In kindle â€” grouped with sibling temp file prefixes
   483â†’ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_Â«COMMANDÂ»_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/Â«prefixÂ»_Â«commandÂ»_"
   484â†’
   485â†’# In command function â€” integer discriminator ensures uniqueness
   486â†’local z_index=0
   487â†’while IFS='|' read -r z_varname z_image z_tag || test -n "${z_varname}"; do
   488â†’  local z_manifest_file="${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_Â«COMMANDÂ»_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
   489â†’  local z_stderr_file="${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_Â«COMMANDÂ»_PREFIX}${z_index}_inspect_stderr.txt"
   490â†’
   491â†’  docker manifest inspect "${z_image}:${z_tag}" \
   492â†’    > "${z_manifest_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
   493â†’    || buc_die "Failed to inspect ${z_image}:${z_tag} â€” see ${z_stderr_file}"
   494â†’
   495â†’  z_index=$((z_index + 1))
   496â†’done
   497â†’```
   498â†’
   499â†’### String Usage
   500â†’
   501â†’```bash
   502â†’# Single-line HERE-string allowed for piping variables
   503â†’jq '.field' <<<"${z_json_data}"
   504â†’grep "pattern" <<<"${z_content}"
   505â†’
   506â†’# Multi-line HERE-strings and HERE-docs prohibited
   507â†’```

   509â†’## Special Function Definitions
   510â†’
   511â†’### Special Function Patterns
   512â†’
   513â†’This table defines scope: Â«prefixÂ»_* is public, zÂ«prefixÂ»_* is internal.
   514â†’
   515â†’| Type      | Pattern                        | First Line           | Returns                      | buc_step                  | Contract                            |
   516â†’|-----------|--------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------------|
   517â†’| Predicate | `[z]Â«prefixÂ»_Â«nameÂ»_predicate` | `zÂ«prefixÂ»_sentinel` | 0=true, 1=false              | No (use buc_log_Â«sourceÂ») | Never dies, status only             |
   518â†’| Capture   | `[z]Â«prefixÂ»_Â«nameÂ»_capture`   | `zÂ«prefixÂ»_sentinel` | stdout once at end or exit 1 | No (use buc_log_Â«sourceÂ») | Clean error handling, single return |
   519â†’| Enroll    | `[z]Â«prefixÂ»_[Â«scopeÂ»_]enroll` | `zÂ«prefixÂ»_sentinel` | `z_Â«funcnameÂ»_Â«retvalÂ»` vars | No (use buc_log_Â«sourceÂ») | Mutates rolls (parallel arrays) in kindle only; returns via variables |
   520â†’| Recite    | `[z]Â«prefixÂ»_Â«whatÂ»_recite`    | `zÂ«prefixÂ»_sentinel` | stdout or exit 1             | No (use buc_log_Â«sourceÂ») | Read-only access to rolls; never mutates |
   521â†’| Tsuite init | `zÂ«tbÂ»_Â«nameÂ»_tsuite_init`  | â€”                    | 0=proceed, non-zero=skip     | No                        | Parent layer; no kindle, no source  |
   522â†’| Tsuite setup | `zÂ«tbÂ»_Â«nameÂ»_tsuite_setup` | â€”                   | â€” (side effects only)        | Optional                  | Suite subshell; kindle, source, configure |
   523â†’| Tcase     | `Â«tcÂ»_Â«nameÂ»_tcase`           | â€”                    | â€” (exit status to suite)     | No                        | Case subshell; assertions, verification |
   524â†’
   525â†’---
   526â†’
   527â†’#### Capture Functions (controlled command substitution)
   528â†’
   529â†’**Purpose: Return a single processed value through stdout with clean error handling.**
   530â†’
   531â†’Capture functions encapsulate command substitution to ensure predictable error handling and single-point-of-return semantics.
   532â†’
   533â†’```bash
   534â†’zÂ«prefixÂ»_Â«nameÂ»_capture() {
   535â†’  zÂ«prefixÂ»_sentinel
   536â†’
   537â†’  # Process and validate internally
   538â†’  local z_result
   539â†’  z_result=$(some_command | process) || return 1
   540â†’
   541â†’  # Validate without buc_die
   542â†’  test -n "${z_result}" || return 1
   543â†’
   544â†’  # Single output at end
   545â†’  echo "${z_result}"
   546â†’}
   547â†’
   548â†’# Caller uses two-line pattern
   549â†’local z_value
   550â†’z_value=$(zÂ«prefixÂ»_Â«nameÂ»_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture value"
   551â†’```
   552â†’
   553â†’**Use capture functions for:**
   554â†’- Secrets that must never touch disk
   555â†’- Values requiring command substitution or pipeline processing
   556â†’- Any computed value where clean error handling matters
   557â†’
   558â†’**Contract:**
   559â†’- Returns stdout once at end or exits with status 1
   560â†’- Never uses `buc_die` internally
   561â†’- No side effects beyond the returned value
   562â†’- May source credential files when necessary for security
   563â†’- May use `buc_log_Â«sourceÂ»` for forensic trail (writes to transcript file only)
   564â†’- Never writes secrets to disk
   565â†’- Never writes to stderr
   566â†’
   567â†’#### Predicate Functions (status only, no output)
   568â†’
   569â†’Predicate functions are used to make decisions, not indicating error.
   570â†’
   571â†’```bash
   572â†’zÂ«prefixÂ»_file_valid_predicate() {
   573â†’  zÂ«prefixÂ»_sentinel
   574â†’
   575â†’  local z_file="${1:-}"
   576â†’
   577â†’  # Return status only - no output, no dies
   578â†’  test -f "${z_file}" || return 1
   579â†’  grep -q "required_pattern" "${z_file}" || return 1
   580â†’
   581â†’  return 0
   582â†’}
   583â†’
   584â†’# Caller uses in conditionals
   585â†’zÂ«prefixÂ»_file_valid_predicate "${z_config}" || buc_warn "Config invalid, using defaults"
   586â†’```
   587â†’
   588â†’#### Enroll Functions (kindle-only registry population)
   589â†’
   590â†’**Purpose**: Populate parallel-array registries (rolls) during module initialization. Enroll functions solve the subshell problem: when a function both mutates shared state and returns a value, calling it in `$()` loses the mutations.
   591â†’
   592â†’**The parallel-array registry pattern (rolls)**:
   593â†’- Kindle initializes N empty arrays (rolls) of the same length
   594â†’- Enroll validates inputs and appends atomically to all rolls
   595â†’- Recite functions (see below) provide read-only access after kindle completes
   596â†’- Variable naming: `z_Â«prefixÂ»_Â«nameÂ»_roll` for all roll arrays (internal state, initialized in kindle)
   597â†’
   598â†’**Example** (using synthetic notation â€” Â«regÂ» owns the rolls, Â«conÂ» consumes them):
   599â†’

600â†’```bash
   601â†’# Module Â«regÂ» â€” owns the rolls
   602â†’zÂ«regÂ»_kindle() {
   603â†’  test -z "${ZÂ«REGÂ»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"
   604â†’
   605â†’  z_Â«regÂ»_name_roll=()
   606â†’  z_Â«regÂ»_target_roll=()
   607â†’  z_Â«regÂ»_handler_roll=()
   608â†’
   609â†’  ZÂ«REGÂ»_KINDLED=1   # MUST be last â€” sentinel guards all subsequent calls
   610â†’}
   611â†’
   612â†’# Enroll function â€” called by consuming module's kindle, NOT Â«regÂ»'s own kindle
   613â†’Â«regÂ»_enroll() {
   614â†’  zÂ«regÂ»_sentinel   # Safe: Â«regÂ» is already kindled when consumer calls this
   615â†’
   616â†’  local z_name="${1:-}"
   617â†’  local z_target="${2:-}"
   618â†’  local z_handler="${3:-}"
   619â†’  test -n "${z_name}" || buc_die "enroll: name required"
   620â†’
   621â†’  # Registration-time validation
   622â†’  declare -F "${z_handler}" >/dev/null || buc_die "enroll: handler not found: ${z_handler}"
   623â†’
   624â†’  z_Â«regÂ»_name_roll+=("${z_name}")
   625â†’  z_Â«regÂ»_target_roll+=("${z_target}")
   626â†’  z_Â«regÂ»_handler_roll+=("${z_handler}")
   627â†’
   628â†’  # Return via variable â€” NOT echo
   629â†’  z_Â«regÂ»_enroll_name="${z_name}"
   630â†’}
   631â†’
   632â†’# Module Â«conÂ» â€” consumes the registry
   633â†’zÂ«conÂ»_kindle() {
   634â†’  test -z "${ZÂ«CONÂ»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"
   635â†’
   636â†’  # Â«regÂ» must be kindled first (rolls initialized, sentinel passes)
   637â†’  Â«regÂ»_enroll "alpha" "/path/alpha" "handle_alpha"
   638â†’  ZÂ«CONÂ»_DEFAULT="${z_Â«regÂ»_enroll_name}"
   639â†’
   640â†’  Â«regÂ»_enroll "bravo" "/path/bravo" "handle_bravo"
   641â†’
   642â†’  ZÂ«CONÂ»_KINDLED=1   # MUST be last
   643â†’}
   644â†’```
   645â†’
   646â†’**Key constraints**:
   647â†’- Enroll functions may ONLY be called within kindle
   648â†’- Rolls are initialized in kindle, populated by enroll in kindle, then immutable after kindle completes
   649â†’- Enroll functions work exclusively with rolls (parallel arrays) â€” no other shared state mutation
   650â†’- Registration-time validation: check invariants at enroll time, not at recite/execution time
   651â†’- Return values use `z_Â«funcnameÂ»_Â«retvalÂ»` convention (function name embedded verbatim for traceability)
   652â†’- Must NOT be called inside `$()` â€” side effects would be lost
   653â†’- May use `buc_die` internally (unlike `_capture`)
   654â†’
   655â†’**Two-level registries**: When entities have parent-child relationships (e.g., groups containing items), use two flat registries with a foreign-key column rather than per-parent dynamic arrays. This avoids `eval` and stays bash 3.2 safe.
   656â†’
   657â†’**Contract summary**:
   658â†’- Mutates rolls (parallel arrays) only
   659â†’- Returns value(s) via `z_Â«funcnameÂ»_Â«retvalÂ»` variables (NOT echo)
   660â†’- Called exclusively within kindle
   661â†’- Should rarely be used â€” only when a function must both mutate and return
   662â†’
   663â†’#### Recite Functions (read-only roll access)
   664â†’
   665â†’**Purpose: Provide read-only access to roll arrays populated by enroll functions.**
   666â†’
   667â†’Recite functions are the only access path to rolls after kindle completes. They must never mutate roll arrays.
   668â†’
   669â†’```bash
   670â†’Â«prefixÂ»_Â«whatÂ»_recite() {
   671â†’  zÂ«prefixÂ»_sentinel
   672â†’
   673â†’  local z_key="${1:-}"
   674â†’  test -n "${z_key}" || return 1
   675â†’
   676â†’  local z_i=0
   677â†’  for z_i in "${!z_Â«prefixÂ»_name_roll[@]}"; do
   678â†’    test "${z_Â«prefixÂ»_name_roll[$z_i]}" = "${z_key}" || continue
   679â†’    echo "${z_Â«prefixÂ»_target_roll[$z_i]}" || return 1
   680â†’    return 0
   681â†’  done
   682â†’  return 1
   683â†’}
   684â†’
   685â†’# Caller uses two-line capture pattern
   686â†’local z_target
   687â†’z_target=$(Â«prefixÂ»_target_recite "alpha") || buc_die "not found"
   688â†’```
   689â†’
   690â†’**Contract:**
   691â†’- Read-only: MUST NOT mutate roll arrays or any shared state
   692â†’- Returns via echo (like capture functions) â€” safe to call in `$()`
   693â†’- Returns 1 on not-found (like `_capture`) â€” never uses `buc_die` internally
   694â†’- Caller decides error handling: `|| buc_die` or conditional
   695â†’- Usable anywhere after kindle completes (not restricted to kindle)
   696â†’
   697â†’---
   698â†’
   699â†’## When to Use Special Functions
   700â†’
   701â†’- **_capture**: Need a value that requires command substitution or clean error handling
   702â†’- **_predicate**: Need true/false for conditional logic without dying
   703â†’- **_enroll**: Populate parallel-array registries (rolls) during kindle with validated inputs and return values
   704â†’- **_recite**: Read-only access to roll arrays populated by enroll functions
   705â†’- **_tsuite_init**: Suite precondition check in parent shell (return non-zero to skip)
   706â†’- **_tsuite_setup**: Suite initialization inside `_tsuite` subshell (kindle, source, configure)
   707â†’- **_tcase**: Test case verification function inside `_tcase` subshell
   708â†’- **Neither**: Use temp files for multi-step pipelines, direct `|| buc_die` for simple failures
   709â†’

   742â†’
   743â†’## Runtime Visibility vs Comments
   744â†’
   745â†’### âś… Use Runtime Visibility
   746â†’
   747â†’```bash
   748â†’# âś… Use Runtime Visibility
   749â†’buc_step "Fetching manifest from registry"          # User milestones
   750â†’buc_log_args "Processing platform linux/amd64"      # Variable details
   751â†’jq '.response' file.json | buc_log_pipe            # Pipeline output
   752â†’
   753â†’# When to use each source:
   754â†’buc_log_args - Simple messages, variable values, progress markers
   755â†’buc_log_pipe - Command output, JSON responses, multi-line data
   756â†’```
   757â†’
   758â†’### âťŚ Never Use Comments for Runtime Info
   759â†’
   760â†’```bash
   761â†’# Building JWT header         âťŚ Wrong
   762â†’buc_log_args "Building JWT header"  âś… Correct
   763â†’
   764â†’# Process each manifest       âťŚ Wrong
   765â†’buc_step "Processing manifests" âś… Correct
   766â†’```
   767â†’
   768â†’Comments are ONLY for code clarification that doesn't matter at runtime.
   769â†’

   771â†’
   772â†’## Test vs Bracket Expressions
   773â†’
   774â†’### âś… Use test Command instead of `[[ xxx ]]/fi` where possible
   775â†’
   776â†’```bash
   777â†’# Error, warning, and optional reporting
   778â†’test -f "${z_file}" || buc_die "File not found"
   779â†’test "${z_var}" = "expected" || buc_warn "Unexpected value"
   780â†’test -n "${z_param}" || buc_step "Running with zero length parameter"
   781â†’```
   782â†’
   783â†’### âś… Exception: Pattern Matching Only
   784â†’
   785â†’```bash
   786â†’# Only acceptable use of [[ ]] - pattern matching requires it
   787â†’[[ "${z_filename}" =~ \.tar\.gz$ ]] && z_extension="tar.gz"
   788â†’```
   789â†’
   790â†’### âťŚ Anti-Patterns
   791â†’
   792â†’```bash
   793â†’# Avoid [[ ]] for simple tests
   794â†’[[ -f "${z_file}" ]]           # Use: test -f "${z_file}"
   795â†’[[ "${z_var}" == "value" ]]    # Use: test "${z_var}" = "value"
   796â†’[[ -n "${z_param}" ]]          # Use: test -n "${z_param}"
   797â†’
   798â†’# Never use test with command substitution that might expand to empty
   799â†’# test with ZERO arguments returns TRUE (exit 0) â€” silent pass landmine

800â†’test $(echo "${z_val}" | grep -E '^pattern$')  # âťŚ Succeeds when grep matches nothing!
   801â†’
   802â†’# âś… Use exit-status predicates instead
   803â†’echo "${z_val}" | grep -qE '^pattern$' || buc_die "Invalid format"
   804â†’
   805â†’# âś… Or use case statement for pattern matching
   806â†’case "${z_val}" in
   807â†’  *pattern*) buc_log_args "Found pattern" ;;
   808â†’  *) buc_die "Pattern not found" ;;
   809â†’esac
   810â†’```
   811â†’
   812â†’**Note**: Use `=` not `==` in test expressions for POSIX compliance.
   813â†’
   814â†’### âťŚ Subshell Exit vs Brace-Group
   815â†’
   816â†’```bash
   817â†’# âťŚ exit in subshell kills only the subshell â€” script continues
   818â†’some_cmd || (echo "ERROR" >&2 && exit 1)
   819â†’
   820â†’# âś… Brace-group stays in same process â€” exit/return works correctly
   821â†’some_cmd || { echo "ERROR" >&2; exit 1; }
   822â†’```
   823â†’
   824â†’**Rule**: Error blocks must use `{ ...; }` not `( ... )` when intending to exit or return. The `(subshell)` creates a new process â€” `exit` terminates only that subshell, not the calling script.
   825â†’
   826â†’### âś… Legitimate Subshell Uses
   827â†’
   828â†’The prohibition above is specific to error-handling blocks where `exit`/`return` must reach the calling shell. Subshells are legitimate in these contexts:
   829â†’
   830â†’- **Test execution** â€” principled isolation between suites and cases; see **Test Execution Patterns**
   831â†’- **`$()` command substitution** â€” inside `_capture` functions only; see **Capture Functions**
   832â†’- **Isolation subshells** â€” environment containment with exit-status propagation (below)
   833â†’
   834â†’#### Isolation Subshells (environment containment)
   835â†’
   836â†’When a sequence of commands mutates the shell environment (sourcing files, `cd`, venv activation, `export`), a subshell prevents those mutations from leaking into subsequent commands. The exit status propagates across the `)` boundary, so the caller can detect and handle failures.
   837â†’
   838â†’**Rarely needed.** Most code operates within a single kindled environment. Use isolation subshells only when you must execute commands that change the environment and those changes must not persist.
   839â†’
   840â†’**Two variants** â€” same principle, different caller handling:
   841â†’
   842â†’```bash
   843â†’# Variant 1: Error propagation â€” die on any internal failure
   844â†’(
   845â†’  source "${z_config_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source: ${z_config_file}"
   846â†’  Â«prefixÂ»_operation || buc_die "Operation failed"
   847â†’) || buc_die "Isolation subshell failed"
   848â†’
   849â†’# Variant 2: Status capture â€” caller inspects and decides
   850â†’local z_status=0
   851â†’(
   852â†’  source "${z_config_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source: ${z_config_file}"
   853â†’  Â«prefixÂ»_operation || buc_die "Operation failed"
   854â†’) || z_status=$?
   855â†’```
   856â†’
   857â†’Inside the subshell, `buc_die` calls `exit` which terminates only the subshell â€” producing a non-zero exit status that the outer `|| buc_die` or `|| z_status=$?` catches. Failure propagates through exit status, not through `exit` reaching the parent.
   858â†’
   859â†’**Constraints:**
   860â†’- Every command inside must have explicit `|| buc_die` â€” `set -e` is suppressed inside `( ... ) ||` (as with all BCG code, never rely on it)
   861â†’- The outer boundary must have `|| buc_die` or `|| z_status=$?` â€” never leave the subshell exit status unchecked
   862â†’- Sourcing inside the subshell is permitted (this is a primary use case â€” isolation prevents sourced variables from leaking)
   863â†’- Keep subshell bodies short and focused â€” if the body grows complex, delegate to a CLI script via `exec`
   864â†’- `$( ... ) || buc_die` is legitimate when you need both isolation and output capture â€” `$()` is a subshell, so isolation holds and the two-line capture pattern applies
   865â†’
   866â†’**Use cases:**
   867â†’- Iterating config files that define overlapping variables (e.g., sourcing each nameplate `.env` in a loop)
   868â†’- Running commands that require `cd` to a different directory
   869â†’- Activating a virtual environment for a single operation
   870â†’- Any sequence where environment mutations must not outlive the block
   871â†’
   872â†’**Anti-pattern â€” no internal error handling:**
   873â†’
   874â†’```bash
   875â†’# âťŚ Failures inside subshell are silent â€” source or echo can fail undetected
   876â†’(
   877â†’  source "${z_file}"
   878â†’  echo "${SOME_VAR}"
   879â†’) || buc_die "Subshell failed"
   880â†’
   881â†’# âś… Every command explicitly handled
   882â†’(
   883â†’  source "${z_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source: ${z_file}"
   884â†’  echo "${SOME_VAR}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
   885â†’) || buc_die "Subshell failed"
   886â†’```
   887â†’

   917â†’## `set -e` is Not Sufficient
   918â†’
   919â†’**The POSIX suppression rule**: `set -e` is suppressed inside `if`, `while`, `||`, `&&` test expressions, and this propagates through the **entire call tree** of the tested command.
   920â†’
   921â†’```bash
   922â†’# âťŚ set -e suppressed for entire call tree of some_function
   923â†’if some_function; then ...
   924â†’
   925â†’# Even if some_function calls other_function that fails,
   926â†’# set -e will not terminate â€” suppression propagates
   927â†’```
   928â†’
   929â†’**BCG rule: Only `_predicate` functions may appear in `if`/`while` conditions.** All other functions must be invoked as simple commands with explicit `|| buc_die` / `|| return`. This completely prevents the suppression hazard.
   930â†’
   931â†’```bash
   932â†’# âś… Predicate in conditional â€” designed for this, never dies, status only
   933â†’if zÂ«prefixÂ»_ready_predicate; then ...
   934â†’
   935â†’# âś… Regular function â€” explicit error handling, not inside conditional
   936â†’some_function || buc_die "..."
   937â†’
   938â†’# âťŚ Regular function in conditional â€” set -e suppressed for entire call tree
   939â†’if some_function; then ...
   940â†’```
   941â†’
   942â†’**Why BCG's `|| buc_die` discipline works**: It doesn't rely on `set -e` â€” explicit error handling after every command means suppression doesn't matter. The `|| z_status=$?` capture pattern intentionally relies on this suppression behavior.
   943â†’
   944â†’**Loops**: `set -e` does not reliably catch failures inside `for`/`while` loop bodies in bash 3.2. Every command in a loop body must have explicit error handling.
   945â†’
   946â†’The error handling suffix depends on the function type:
   947â†’
   948â†’| Function type    | On failure use       | Why                                             |
   949â†’|------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
   950â†’| Regular/enroll   | `\|\| buc_die`       | Enroll validates invariants; violations are fatal |
   951â†’| Predicate        | `\|\| return 1`      | Never dies, status only                          |
   952â†’| Capture/recite   | `\|\| return 1`      | Never dies, caller decides                       |
   953â†’| Flow control     | `\|\| continue`      | Intentional skip to next iteration               |
   954â†’

   956â†’
   957â†’## Array Safety Under `set -u`
   958â†’
   959â†’Under `set -u`, `"${array[@]}"` on an empty array triggers "unbound variable" in bash 3.2.
   960â†’
   961â†’**Primary pattern: index iteration** â€” works safely on empty arrays, no guard needed:
   962â†’
   963â†’```bash
   964â†’# âś… Safe: index iteration works on empty arrays under set -u
   965â†’for z_i in "${!z_Â«prefixÂ»_name_roll[@]}"; do
   966â†’  echo "${z_Â«prefixÂ»_name_roll[$z_i]}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
   967â†’done
   968â†’```
   969â†’
   970â†’**Acceptable alternative: guarded value iteration** â€” check array is non-empty before expanding. The `(( expr ))` arithmetic command returns exit 0 when expr is non-zero, exit 1 when zero â€” it works in bash 3.2 and is the idiomatic guard for array size checks:
   971â†’
   972â†’```bash
   973â†’# âś… Acceptable: guard value iteration with size check
   974â†’if (( ${#z_Â«prefixÂ»_name_roll[@]} )); then
   975â†’  for z_val in "${z_Â«prefixÂ»_name_roll[@]}"; do
   976â†’    echo "${z_val}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
   977â†’  done
   978â†’fi
   979â†’```
   980â†’
   981â†’**Anti-pattern:**
   982â†’
   983â†’```bash
   984â†’# âťŚ Unsafe: value iteration fails on empty arrays under set -u in bash 3.2
   985â†’for z_val in "${z_Â«prefixÂ»_name_roll[@]}"; do
   986â†’  echo "${z_val}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
   987â†’done
   988â†’```

   991â†’
   992â†’## Stdin Consumption in While-Read Loops
   993â†’
   994â†’### The Problem
   995â†’
   996â†’When `while read ... done < file` feeds a file to stdin (FD 0), **any child process that reads stdin silently consumes the loop's remaining input**. This includes `docker exec -i`, `ssh`, `read` without explicit FD, `cat` without arguments, and any command that inherits stdin.
   997â†’
   998â†’The failure is **silent and partial**: the loop processes some iterations correctly, then stops early with no error. This is one of the hardest bash bugs to diagnose because the loop appears to work â€” it just processes fewer items than expected.
   999â†’

1000â†’### âťŚ Anti-Pattern: Open-FD Loop Body
  1001â†’
  1002â†’```bash
  1003â†’# âťŚ File held open on stdin for entire loop â€” child processes can consume it
  1004â†’while IFS= read -r z_item || test -n "${z_item}"; do
  1005â†’    complex_function "${z_item}"    # If this touches stdin, remaining items vanish
  1006â†’done < "${z_temp_file}"
  1007â†’```
  1008â†’
  1009â†’### âś… Pattern: Load-Then-Iterate
  1010â†’
  1011â†’**Always** read file contents into an array first, then iterate. The file is fully consumed and closed before any loop body code runs.
  1012â†’
  1013â†’```bash
  1014â†’# âś… Load phase â€” file consumed and closed
  1015â†’local z_items=()
  1016â†’while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
  1017â†’    z_items+=("${z_line}")
  1018â†’done < "${z_temp_file}"
  1019â†’rm -f "${z_temp_file}"
  1020â†’
  1021â†’# âś… Iterate phase â€” stdin is free, no FD held open
  1022â†’local z_i
  1023â†’for z_i in "${!z_items[@]}"; do
  1024â†’    test -n "${z_items[$z_i]}" || continue
  1025â†’    complex_function "${z_items[$z_i]}"
  1026â†’done
  1027â†’```

  1031â†’## Sourcing Rules
  1032â†’
  1033â†’Sourcing is restricted because it breaks error handling. Only three locations may source files:
  1034â†’
  1035â†’| Location             | Can Source                 | Purpose                                              |
  1036â†’|----------------------|----------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
  1037â†’| CLI file header      | All dependencies           | Module loading                                       |
  1038â†’| CLI Furnish Function | Config files               | Environment setup                                    |
  1039â†’| Internal functions   | Credentials when necessary | Context-specific secrets (with documented rationale) |
  1040â†’
  1041â†’## Eval Policy
  1042â†’
  1043â†’`eval` is **forbidden** except for validated variable-name dereference. Require `^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$` regex validation before any eval.
  1044â†’
  1045â†’**Prefer indirect expansion for reading** â€” `${!name}` works in bash 3.2:
  1046â†’
  1047â†’```bash
  1048â†’# âś… Indirect expansion for reading
  1049â†’local z_val="${!z_varname}"
  1050â†’```
  1051â†’
  1052â†’**Use `printf -v` for assignment to indirect variable** â€” after name validation:
  1053â†’
  1054â†’```bash
  1055â†’# âś… printf -v for assignment â€” no quoting hazards from values
  1056â†’echo "${z_varname}" | grep -qE '^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$' \
  1057â†’  || buc_die "Invalid variable name: ${z_varname}"
  1058â†’printf -v "${z_varname}" '%s' "${z_new_value}" || buc_die "printf -v failed"
  1059â†’```
  1060â†’
  1061â†’**Anti-patterns:**
  1062â†’
  1063â†’```bash
  1064â†’# âťŚ eval for assignment â€” quoting hazards from values with quotes/newlines/backslashes
  1065â†’eval "${z_varname}=\${z_new_value}"
  1066â†’
  1067â†’# âťŚ Unvalidated eval â€” injection risk
  1068â†’eval "local z_val=\${${z_varname}:-}"
  1069â†’```

  1071â†’## Output and Messaging Patterns
  1072â†’
  1073â†’### Message Hierarchy
  1074â†’
  1075â†’buc_step "Major user-visible operation"     # White, stderr, milestones
  1076â†’buc_log_args "Detail from variables"        # Transcript only, from arguments
  1077â†’command | buc_log_pipe                      # Transcript only, from pipeline
  1078â†’buc_warn "Non-fatal warning"                # Yellow, stderr
  1079â†’buc_die "Fatal error message"               # Red, stderr, exits
  1080â†’
  1081â†’### When to use buc_step vs buc_log_Â«sourceÂ»:
  1082â†’
  1083â†’**Use buc_step in:**
  1084â†’- Public command functions (rbv_start, rbv_stop)
  1085â†’- Top-level user-initiated operations
  1086â†’- Major state transitions visible to user
  1087â†’
  1088â†’**Use buc_log_Â«sourceÂ» in:**
  1089â†’- Internal helper functions (zrbgo_*)
  1090â†’- Capture functions (*_capture) - keeps stdout clean
  1091â†’- Predicate functions (*_predicate) - no user output
  1092â†’- Forensic/debugging information
  1093â†’- Sub-operations within a larger step
  1094â†’
  1095â†’### âś… Code Display
  1096â†’
  1097â†’```bash
  1098â†’buc_code "command-to-run --with-args"       # Cyan, for showing commands
  1099â†’```
  1100â†’
  1101â†’### âťŚ Anti-Patterns
  1102â†’
  1103â†’```bash
  1104â†’# Never use raw echo for user messages
  1105â†’echo "Starting operation..."
  1106â†’
  1107â†’# Never use comments for runtime information
  1108â†’# Processing items    âťŚ
  1109â†’buc_log_args "Processing items"  âś…
  1110â†’
  1111â†’# Never mix stdout and stderr purposes
  1112â†’echo "Result: ${z_value}"          # If this is return data
  1113â†’echo "Progress update..." >&2      # If this is progress info
  1114â†’```

  1162â†’## Module Maturity Checklist
  1163â†’
  1164â†’### Module Structure
  1165â†’- [ ] Implementation file Â«prefixÂ»_Â«nameÂ».sh exists
  1166â†’- [ ] CLI file Â«prefixÂ»_cli.sh exists (skip if library/utility module with no direct user commands)
  1167â†’- [ ] Implementation has multiple inclusion detection guard (`ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_SOURCED`)
  1168â†’- [ ] CLI starts with `set -euo pipefail`
  1169â†’- [ ] Implementation file sources nothing (except within kindle function)
  1170â†’- [ ] CLI file sources all dependencies in header
  1171â†’- [ ] External code accesses module through CLI only (no direct `z*_kindle()` calls from outside)
  1172â†’
  1173â†’### Required Functions
  1174â†’- [ ] `zÂ«prefixÂ»_kindle` - first line: `test -z "${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die`
  1175â†’- [ ] `zÂ«prefixÂ»_sentinel` - first line: `test "${ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die`
  1176â†’- [ ] `zÂ«prefixÂ»_furnish` (CLI only) - documents env vars, sources configs, calls kindle
  1177â†’- [ ] `zÂ«prefixÂ»_enforce` (regime archetype only) - `buv_vet` + custom format checks after kindle
  1178â†’- [ ] All public functions start with sentinel check
  1179â†’- [ ] All internal helpers prefixed with `zÂ«prefixÂ»_`
  1180â†’
  1181â†’### Variable Management
  1182â†’- [ ] All kindle constants (internal `ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_SCREAMING` and public `Â«PREFIXÂ»_SCREAMING`) defined exclusively in kindle
  1183â†’- [ ] No kindle constant assignments outside kindle function
  1184â†’- [ ] Literal constants (`Â«PREFIXÂ»_lower_name`) are pure string literals with no `${}` expansion, placed after `SOURCED` guard
  1185â†’- [ ] All local variables use `z_` prefix
  1186â†’- [ ] All expansions use `"${var}"` pattern (braced, quoted)
  1187â†’- [ ] Parameters use `"${1:-}"` pattern for defensive programming
  1188â†’- [ ] Module state variable `ZÂ«PREFIXÂ»_KINDLED=1` is the last statement in kindle
  1189â†’- [ ] No bare `$var` or unbraced `"$var"` expansions
  1190â†’- [ ] No `local -i` â€” use plain local with explicit validation
  1191â†’- [ ] No raw `eval` for value assignment â€” use `printf -v` after name validation; `${!name}` for reading
  1192â†’
  1193â†’### Error Handling
  1194â†’- [ ] Every command that can fail has `|| buc_die` (`|| buc_warn` only with a human-authored comment granting permission and explaining why non-fatal is safe)
  1195â†’- [ ] `_predicate` functions return 0/1, never die, no output
  1196â†’- [ ] `_capture` functions output once at end or exit 1, no stderr
  1197â†’- [ ] `_enroll` functions set `z_Â«funcnameÂ»_Â«retvalÂ»` return vars, never echo; callers never use `$()` (when applicable)
  1198â†’- [ ] `_enroll` functions called only within kindle (when applicable)
  1199â†’- [ ] `_recite` functions never mutate roll arrays (when applicable)

1200â†’- [ ] Roll arrays use `z_Â«prefixÂ»_Â«nameÂ»_roll` naming convention (when applicable)
  1201â†’- [ ] Two-line pattern for capturing: `z_var=$(func_capture) || buc_die`
  1202â†’- [ ] File reads validated: `test -n "${z_content}" || buc_die`
